questions.question_text: The mass of a body in classical mechanics is best described as which of the following physical quantities relevant to motion in sport?

options.text: A measure of the inertia of a body and amount of matter
The colour pattern visible on the surface of the body
The instantaneous velocity vector of a body in motion
The acceleration produced by gravity on the body only

questions.explanation: Mass is a scalar quantity that measures the amount of matter in a body and serves as a measure of its inertia, that is, its resistance to changes in motion when an external force is applied; it is independent of the body's location.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Surface colour has no direct mechanical relation to mass.
Velocity is a separate kinematic quantity from mass.
Gravitational acceleration acts on a mass; it is not mass itself.

questions.question_text: Friction in classical mechanics is best defined as a force that acts in which of the following ways at the contact between two solid surfaces?

options.text: It always acts perpendicular to the contact surfaces
It acts parallel to the surfaces and opposes relative motion
It acts only when surfaces are not in contact at all
It acts in the same direction as the applied force always

questions.explanation: Friction is a contact force that acts parallel to the surfaces in contact and opposes the relative motion or its tendency between them; it is essential in sport for grip and propulsion as well as for slowing rolling balls and sliding athletes.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Perpendicular to the surface is the normal reaction force.
Friction requires surfaces to be in contact with each other.
Friction always opposes, not assists, the applied motion.

questions.question_text: Which of the following everyday sport situations best demonstrates that friction is sometimes desirable and is increased intentionally by athletes for better performance?

options.text: Cyclist applies oil to the bicycle chain to make it smoother
Cricket bowler rubs the ball on flannels to keep one side shiny
Gymnast applies chalk powder to hands before performing on bars
Skater wears polished steel blades to glide on flat smooth ice

questions.explanation: Gymnasts apply magnesium carbonate (chalk) powder to their hands before performing on the parallel bars or rings; the chalk dries sweat and increases friction, improving grip on the apparatus and reducing the risk of slipping during the routine.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Oil on a chain is meant to reduce friction in cycling.
Shining one side of a cricket ball alters air friction, not gripping.
Polished blades on smooth ice deliberately reduce friction.

questions.question_text: Which of the following sports scenarios best illustrates a case where friction is intentionally reduced to improve athletic performance during competition?

options.text: A football player wears studded boots on the natural grass
A swimmer wears a tight streamlined low-drag swimsuit in the race
A wrestler grips opponents very tightly during a wrestling bout
A basketball player ties shoelaces tightly before the game starts

questions.explanation: A streamlined low-drag swimsuit reduces fluid friction (drag) between the swimmer's body and the surrounding water, allowing the swimmer to glide more efficiently and lose less energy to fluid resistance over the length of the race in the pool.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Studs on football boots are designed to increase friction.
Tight gripping in wrestling intentionally raises friction.
Tight shoelaces secure the foot but do not change friction.

questions.question_text: Which one of the following pairs of variables is directly proportional in Newton's second law of motion when the mass of the body remains constant during analysis?

options.text: Force and acceleration of the body
Force and the time of action only
Acceleration and the colour of the body
Mass and the velocity of the body

questions.explanation: From F = m × a with constant mass, the force is directly proportional to the acceleration produced; doubling the applied force on the same mass doubles its acceleration, which is fundamental to power training and equipment design in sport.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Time of force action relates to impulse, not direct proportionality.
Colour of the body is unrelated to mechanical motion.
Mass and velocity are not proportionally linked through the second law.

questions.question_text: A cricket pitch covered with damp dewy grass tends to increase the rolling distance of a delivered ball compared to a dry pitch; which of the following is the best biomechanical reason?

options.text: Damp grass increases friction between ball and pitch surface
Damp grass reduces friction between ball and pitch surface
Damp grass changes the direction of gravity acting on the ball
Damp grass alters the mass of the cricket ball significantly

questions.explanation: A thin film of moisture acts as a lubricant between the ball and the grass, lowering the effective coefficient of friction; with less friction the ball loses less kinetic energy per unit distance and continues rolling further before coming to a stop.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: If friction increased, the ball would slow down faster, not roll further.
Gravity is unaffected by surface dampness on a cricket pitch.
The mass of a regulation cricket ball does not change with moisture.

questions.question_text: Momentum of a body in classical mechanics is best defined by which of the following expressions used in sports biomechanics analysis?

options.text: Mass divided by velocity of the body
Product of mass and velocity of the body
Mass divided by acceleration of the body
Acceleration divided by mass of the body

questions.explanation: Momentum p is the product of the mass m and the velocity v of a body (p = m × v); a heavy slow rugby player and a lighter faster sprinter can carry similar momentum, which matters during collisions and tackles in many contact sports.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Mass divided by velocity is dimensionally inconsistent with momentum.
Mass divided by acceleration would give force divided by acceleration squared.
Acceleration divided by mass is not momentum at all.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why a cricketer wisely 'gives' with the hands while catching a fast-moving cricket ball during fielding in a competitive match?

options.text: It reduces impact time, increasing the peak force on the hands
It prolongs the impact time, reducing the peak force experienced
It increases the mass of the ball during catching action
It reduces the gravitational pull acting during the catch

questions.explanation: By the impulse–momentum theorem F × t = change in momentum; pulling the hands back during the catch prolongs the impact time t, so for the same change in momentum the peak force F on the hands is reduced, lowering pain and injury risk.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Reducing impact time would raise, not reduce, peak force.
Catching does not change the ball's mass at all.
Gravitational pull is unchanged by hand technique.

questions.question_text: Two skaters of equal mass push each other apart on smooth ice in opposite directions; which physical principle best explains the resulting motion of the two skaters?

options.text: Newton's first law alone, due to inertia of skaters
Newton's third law and conservation of momentum
Gravitational force between the two skaters
Friction between their skates and the ice surface

questions.explanation: By Newton's third law each skater exerts an equal and opposite force on the other; conservation of momentum then gives equal and opposite changes in velocity for skaters of equal mass, sending them apart with equal speeds on the smooth ice surface.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: First law alone does not explain motion produced by the push.
Gravitational attraction between human bodies is negligibly small.
Friction with smooth ice is minimal during the brief push.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the principal reason that car-racing tyres are made wider than ordinary road-vehicle tyres for racing purposes?

options.text: Wider tyres look more attractive to spectators in the stadium
Wider tyres provide a larger contact area, increasing grip and friction
Wider tyres reduce friction and let the car slide easily
Wider tyres make the car body lighter overall during racing

questions.explanation: Wider racing tyres distribute the contact load over a larger area at very high speeds and use special rubber compounds, producing greater grip with the track surface; this increased effective friction helps cornering, acceleration and braking on a race circuit.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Visual appearance is irrelevant to functional racing design.
Reducing friction would worsen high-speed cornering safety.
Wider tyres make the car heavier, not lighter, by themselves.

questions.question_text: Match each common sports practice or piece of equipment in Column A with the type of friction it is mainly designed to manage according to Column B and choose the right combination.

column_a.text: Rosin powder on baseball pitcher's hand

column_b.text: Increases sliding friction with the playing surface
Reduces sliding friction on smooth ice surface
Increases grip with the cricket or baseball

questions.explanation: Studs increase sliding friction with grass; polished blades reduce friction on ice; streamlined helmet shape reduces fluid friction (air drag) in cycling; rosin powder dries sweat and increases grip with the ball — four distinct friction-management strategies in sport.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Studs are universally a friction-increasing design feature.
Streamlining always targets fluid (air or water) friction.
Powders on hands always aim to improve gripping friction.

questions.question_text: Match each mechanical concept in Column A with its correct definition or formula listed in Column B and choose the option showing the right combination of these two columns.

column_b.text: Product of force and time interval
Measure of the inertia of a body
Mass times acceleration of a body

questions.explanation: Mass is the measure of the body's inertia; force is mass times acceleration (F = ma); momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = m × v); impulse is the product of force and the time interval over which it acts (J = F × t).
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Mass is the measure of inertia of any given body.
Force is precisely m × a in classical mechanics.
Impulse always equals force multiplied by the time of action.

questions.question_text: Match each sport-specific situation in Column A with the most relevant Newton's law or related principle from Column B and choose the correct combination.

column_a.text: Heavy shot needs more force than baseball
Sprinter pushes blocks; blocks push sprinter

questions.explanation: Catching with give applies impulse–momentum reasoning; head-on billiard ball collision is governed by conservation of linear momentum; heavier shot needing more force fits F = ma directly; sprinter–block interaction illustrates Newton's third law of action-reaction.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Catching with give is a classic impulse-momentum example.
Head-on collisions are analysed using momentum conservation.
F = ma directly governs force-mass-acceleration relationships.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about friction in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in athletic equipment design.
questions.assertion: An ice-skater wears polished steel blades to glide more easily on the smooth ice during a performance routine.
questions.reason: Polished steel blades on smooth ice produce a very low coefficient of friction, allowing the skater to slide with little resistance.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Polished steel blades and smooth ice form a contact pair with very low coefficient of friction; the skater therefore meets little resistance to motion and can glide easily across the rink, directly explaining the assertion through the friction mechanism.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason gives the correct friction mechanism for skating.
Both statements are well-established in skating biomechanics.
Low friction directly enables easy gliding across ice.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about momentum in sport and choose the option that best represents their logical connection in collision biomechanics.
questions.assertion: When a small fast cricket ball strikes a stationary heavy bowling ball, the cricket ball can rebound back along its original path.
questions.reason: In an elastic collision between two bodies of very unequal mass, the lighter body tends to bounce back while the heavier body barely moves.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Conservation of momentum and elastic collision analysis show that when a lighter body collides head-on with a much heavier stationary body, the lighter one rebounds with almost reversed velocity while the heavier body gains only a small velocity — exactly the assertion's outcome.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason gives the correct elastic collision mechanism.
Both statements follow from momentum conservation.
The lighter body's rebound is well-supported by physics.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason regarding rolling friction in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in athletic surface design.
questions.assertion: A bowling ball rolls a long way down a smooth hardwood lane in a bowling alley, even with little additional energy input.
questions.reason: Rolling friction between a hard rolling sphere and a hard polished surface is generally much smaller than sliding friction between the same surfaces.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Rolling friction between hard surfaces is much smaller than sliding friction; on a polished bowling lane, the rolling ball loses kinetic energy slowly and travels a long way, so the reason directly explains the long roll observed in bowling alleys.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Rolling friction is genuinely smaller than sliding friction.
Both statements are well-established in classical mechanics.
Low rolling friction directly produces long rolling distances.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following events in the chronological order in which momentum and friction principles act when a footballer kicks a ball that finally rolls to a stop on the field.

items.text: Foot transfers momentum to the football at impact
Ball is at rest with static friction holding it
Rolling friction gradually reduces the ball's momentum
Ball stops as its kinetic energy is fully dissipated

options.text: Q → P → R → S
P → Q → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → S → Q

questions.explanation: The football starts at rest under static friction; the foot transfers momentum at impact, setting it rolling; rolling friction with the grass gradually reduces its momentum; finally the ball stops once its kinetic energy is fully dissipated by friction.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Static friction acts before any kicking force is applied.
Momentum transfer must precede the rolling phase.
The complete stop is always the final event.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following stages in the correct chronological order during a head-on tackle between two rugby players in a competitive match using Newton's laws.

items.text: Each player applies a force on the other on contact
Both players run toward each other with momentum
Equal and opposite reaction forces act between them
Total momentum redistributes after the collision

options.text: Q → P → R → S
P → Q → R → S
R → S → P → Q
S → R → Q → P

questions.explanation: Players first run toward each other carrying their individual momentum; on contact each applies a force on the other; by Newton's third law equal and opposite reaction forces act between them; finally total momentum redistributes after the collision.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Approach phase precedes the contact between players.
Action force precedes the resulting reaction force.
Momentum redistribution is the closing phase of the collision.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: Friction between an athlete's foot and the ground is always undesirable and should be eliminated in every sport at all times.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Friction is essential in many sports such as sprinting, football and basketball where grip with the ground enables propulsion and quick changes of direction; it is reduced only in selected situations such as ice-skating where gliding is the goal.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Friction is essential for propulsion in running and team sports.
Eliminating all friction would make sprinting impossible.
Friction is reduced selectively for activities like skating.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram comparing the magnitudes of static, sliding and rolling friction for the same pair of surfaces and identify which type produces the largest force opposing motion.

options.text: Limiting static friction
Sliding (kinetic) friction
Rolling friction
Fluid friction in a vacuum chamber

questions.explanation: For the same pair of surfaces under similar conditions, the limiting static friction is the largest of the three types; sliding friction is slightly smaller, and rolling friction is the smallest, which is why it takes more effort to start an object moving than to keep it sliding or rolling.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Sliding friction is smaller than the limiting static friction.
Rolling friction is the smallest of the three types.
Fluid friction in a vacuum is essentially zero by definition.
